primary_diagnosis.name: Acute panic attack triggered by an incidental early primary biliary cholangitis (AMA-positive) result; no acute liver pathology

comorbidities.name: Early primary biliary cholangitis (AMA-positive, structurally normal liver)

pathophysiology_summary: Early primary biliary cholangitis (positive antimitochondrial antibody, normal/slightly raised ALP, no pruritus/jaundice, structurally normal liver) is a very slowly progressive autoimmune small-bile-duct condition that takes years-to-decades to cause significant disease and is highly manageable (e.g. ursodeoxycholic acid). Reading about it triggered a panic attack with palpitations, hyperventilation, derealisation and a gripping RUQ tightness, with fear of hepatic coma. The genuine emergencies to exclude — fulminant hepatic failure/encephalopathy (jaundice, confusion, asterixis, bleeding) and biliary sepsis (fever, severe pain) — are all absent, and are screenable by history/appearance over telehealth. Management is calm accurate reassurance and de-escalation with a liver safety-net.

differential.diagnosis: Panic attack over incidental early PBC
differential.evidence_basis: Palpitations, hyperventilation and derealisation triggered by researching an incidental result, with prior health anxiety.
Screen for jaundice, confusion, asterixis, bleeding; absent.
Screen for fever and severe RUQ pain; absent.
Tightness is panic-associated and non-exertional/non-radiating; low probability but retain a cardiac safety-net.

differential.diagnosis: Panic attack over incidental early PBC
differential.evidence_basis: No jaundice/encephalopathy/bleeding (DI-001), no fever and only mild panic-associated RUQ tightness (DI-002), trigger-locked onset with derealisation and prior anxiety (DI-003) — a panic attack.
Clear mentation, anicteric, no bleeding; early PBC does not fail overnight (safety-net retained).
Afebrile, soft mild tightness — biliary sepsis not supported.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correct output: accurate reassurance (early PBC is slow/benign, not failing tonight), a jaundice/encephalopathy and biliary-sepsis screen, panic de-escalation, routine GP/hepatology follow-up, and a liver safety-net. Both reflex ED and unscreened dismissal are errors.

red_flags.description: Hepatic decompensation — jaundice, confusion/drowsiness, asterixis, or bleeding/bruising
Biliary sepsis — fever/rigors with severe RUQ pain
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed cholangitis progresses to septic shock.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Being drawn into the 'liver coma' fear and dispatching to ED for a well, anicteric, clear-minded patient.

telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Reassure (early PBC is slow/benign) with a jaundice/encephalopathy and biliary-sepsis screen; panic de-escalation; routine GP/hepatology follow-up; liver safety-net.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: False reassurance could miss a (rare here) decompensation; over-triage reinforces catastrophic anxiety.
